title: RA.1013 Kilopatrah Jones
tracklist:
Rinor & Lotenna - Custom RA Intro
Ruben Carrera - Sexuality (Lovelace Mix)
Ralphi Rosario - Brujeria (Witchcraft)
Tronco Traxx - Walk 4 Me (Lotties Vogue Mix)
Jimmy Somerville - Heartbeat (Armand's Cardiac Mix)
Bump - Im Stompin' (Bumpin Mix)
Gwen Dickey & KWS - Ain't Nobody (Junior Vasquez Sound Factory Vocal Mix)
RuPaul - Supermodel (Alternative Mix)
Virtual Soul - Luvin' (Whole Lotta Luv)
S.R.-1 - Da Rhythm (Strictly Rhythm Mix)
Karizma - Spirit
Louie Vega - How He Works (N-You-Up Remix)
Geoffrey C - This Is Hot (Yes Indeedy)
Zach Witness - Let House Reign (Accapella)
The Coach House - Time Warp
Kamasutra - Express (Train Version)
SWEET PUSSY PAULINE - U bean Eatin' (Divaepella EDIT)
Donna Summer - I Feel Love (Remastered)
Celine Dion - Misled (Richie Jones Club Mix)
CeCe Peniston - Lifeline
Madonna - Rescue Me (Titanic Vocal)

translation.content: Ask Kilopatrah Jones who their favourite DJ is and they'll give you an unequivocal answer: Junior Vasquez. The influence is telling. Both born and raised in Queens, the two artists share a sense of house as a life force. Not in a New Age, spiritual sense. For Jones, it means being "sweaty, panting and free." It's a lineage that reaches back to the late '80s and '90s, when New York's house and ballroom scenes offered a vital refuge for the city's queer and Black communities. Jones arrives to this heritage knowingly, and that history courses through RA.1013 from the jump. After a tongue-in-cheek custom intro, we're plunged straight into house's queer vocabulary: Ruben Carrera's "Sexuality," Ralphi Rosario's "Brujeria," Tronco Traxx's "Walk 4 Me." "I'm a house dancer first, raised by Paradise Garage and Sound Factory heads," they tell us in the Q&A below. "It's definitely for the gworls and divas first." The New York DJ can play anything from techno to drum & bass, but when playing house, there's a particular sense of clarity: they understand the genre in the same way as Vasquez and his peers–as a space for reinvention, transgression, glamour and joy. Across the following two hours, RA.1013 immerses you in that world. It feels like walking into the Sound Factory at peak time, bodies in glorious motion, fog thick in the air. Jones runs through familiar classics, bringing it home with a gag-inducing sweep of Donna Summer, CeCe Peniston, Celine Dion and, fittingly, Madonna—a wink to Jones's hero and his defining moment. RA.1013 reasserts the idea that clubs should be a place of joy. Not only for our individual needs and fulfilment, but as a political act—especially for marginalised folk. Arriving at a time when systemic regression and cultural decay feels increasingly pervasive, Jones is a model for what DJs and dance floors can, and arguably should, be. As they put it, "I'm just so happy and grateful to be alive and in this body." - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Girl, everything! This year I toured Europe for the third time, and at home I shared the stage with some of my heroes, including Danny Tenaglia and Honey Dijon. These experiences opened up my world and truly inspired me. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? I recorded this in the cosy, beautiful Queens studio I share with my mentor, Dee Diggs. With this mix, I wanted to centre pleasure as a right. Being a fat, Black femme, centreing hedonism has given me power against systems that reject this and reject me. I wanted it to be horny, sexy, flirty—everything I want to hear while I'm on the dance floor: sweaty, panting and free. I'm a house dancer first, raised by Paradise Garage and Sound Factory heads, so I'd say it's definitely for the gworls and divas first. So: diva house and horniness, simply put. ⁠What are you looking forward to in the near future? REST!! With winter coming soon and spending more time at home, I'm trying to be more intentional with my free time. I want to pour it back into myself, create more space to rest, and move with a little more presence instead of urgency. I'm beginning my first DJ residency in January. I feel very vulnerable about it, so I may be over-preparing, but I'm excited. Besides that, I'm preparing for my upcoming spring EU tour in 2026. With the experience I've gained, I know it's going to be life-changing hearing those record rips on a modern, booming sound system. I got my first taste of festival season, and honestly, I'm hungry for more. Lastly, I'm manifesting a b2b with Kerri Chandler—Kerri, if you see this, email me, twin! What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I wish the world paid more attention to the politics of joy—to how freedom, creativity and community can be acts of survival. I come from a small Haitian family shaped by war and politics, and because of that, I grew up with a deep understanding that yes, life is fragile. However, it's also meant to be lived fully. My family always emphasised showing up for life today, not waiting for the perfect moment to exist, to love or to express yourself. I honestly feel that lesson lives in the club for me. The dance floor is a space where I can be curious and rebuild myself outside of social confines. It's where I can explore identity, sensuality and connection without judgment. I might look like I'm drunk, dancing by myself, but genuinely, I'm just so happy and grateful to be alive and in this body. I think people often overlook how radical that freedom is—especially for those of us who carry histories of displacement or marginalisation. To dance, to feel joy and to exist loudly in our bodies is its own form of resistance. For others, it's just clubbing, but for me? It's liberation in motion. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Body Language's second anniversary at Re:mise in Berlin completely cracked me open. Stella Zekri—who I met through Dee (thank you, Dee!)—booked me for my first truly international party, and it changed everything. As a New Yorker, it was the furthest I'd ever been from home, yet that night felt deeply familiar. The energy was electric—like, close your eyes and imagine a raw, communal, alive party. I swear I saw five non-binary people jacking in a row; it was glorious and free. I met the rest of the Body Language crew—Elliot, Caitlin, Brüik, and Camilla—that night, people who have since become my chosen family. Even without speaking a word of German, the music spoke for me. Honestly, my diva house set didn't stand a chance against those girls; their energy pushed me to loosen my grip, listen deeply and let the music lead.
translation.blurb: A lesson in "diva house and horniness" from a modern New York darling.
